title: ASP.NET Core Big Picture: A Practical Developer Guide
excerpt: A simple and practical overview of ASP.NET Core: what it is, how it works, MVC vs Razor Pages vs Blazor, Web API vs gRPC, and where SignalR fits.

body.value:
ASP.NET Core is Microsoft's web framework for building web applications on .NET.

The easiest way to understand it is this: it provides ready-to-use building blocks so you don’t have to implement everything from scratch.

It is not just about generating HTML pages. It supports multiple application styles including front-end UI, APIs, and real-time communication.

value.title: Key idea
value.text: ASP.NET Core is a single platform that supports multiple ways of building web applications.

value.caption: ASP.NET Core overview
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[ASP.NET Core] --> B[Server-rendered UI]
    A --> C[Client-rendered UI]
    A --> D[Back-end APIs]
    A --> E[Real-time features]
    B --> B1[MVC]
    B --> B2[Razor Pages]
    C --> C1[Blazor]
    D --> D1[Web API]
    D --> D2[gRPC]
    E --> E1[SignalR]

body.value:
A static website serves fixed content like HTML and CSS. A real application needs more: data access, business logic, validation, and interaction.

ASP.NET Core exists to provide a structured way to build such applications without reinventing core functionality like routing, dependency injection, or request handling.

• Cross-platform (Windows, Linux)
  • Open source
  • High performance
  • Built on .NET and C#
  • Modular (features are opt-in)
  • Shared foundation across all application styles

value.title: How every ASP.NET Core app starts

value.caption: Application startup

body.value:
Core concepts:

  • Dependency Injection via builder.Services
  • Middleware pipeline via app.Use*
  • Routing via app.Map*

value.caption: Request flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Browser] --> B[Kestrel]
    B --> C[Middleware]
    C --> D[Routing]
    D --> E[Handler]
    E --> F[Response]

body.value:
Kestrel is the built-in web server. In production, it is often placed behind IIS or NGINX.

Middleware defines how requests are processed. Order matters because each step runs sequentially.

ASP.NET Core uses built-in dependency injection.

  • Singleton - one instance for entire app
  • Scoped - one per request
  • Transient - new instance each time
Razor allows combining HTML and C# using the @ syntax. It is used in Razor Pages, MVC, and Blazor.

value.caption: Server-rendered flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    Browser->>Server: Request
    Server->>Database: Load data
    Server-->>Browser: HTML

body.value:
Server-rendered applications generate HTML on the server and send it to the browser.

Razor Pages is page-focused and simpler. MVC separates logic into controllers, models, and views.

value.caption: Blazor models
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Blazor] --> B[WebAssembly]
    A --> C[Server]

body.value:
Blazor allows building interactive UI using C#.

  • WebAssembly runs in browser
  • Server runs on server via SignalR

Web API is used to expose data via HTTP using JSON.

  • GET - read
  • POST - create
  • PUT - update

gRPC uses contracts and binary communication for high performance.

It is typically used for internal communication between services.

SignalR enables real-time communication between server and clients using persistent connections.

It is commonly used for live updates like dashboards or notifications.

ASP.NET Core is one platform with multiple approaches to solving different problems.

All application types share the same foundation: middleware, dependency injection, and routing.

Once the core concepts are understood, moving between different parts of ASP.NET Core becomes much easier.
